title: Subword Vocabulary Tradeoffs
concept: Choosing vocabulary size balances sequence length (smaller vocab means longer token lists) against model parameters. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Recurrent Neural Networks.'

exercises.prompt: Before we balance tradeoffs, what does a subword tokenizer split text into?
exercises.explain: Subwords are pieces of words (like 'un', 'break', 'able'). They help models handle new words without needing an infinite dictionary.

cards.title: Finding the Sweet Spot
cards.body: Have you ever wondered why AI models don't just memorize every single word in existence? If we give them a dictionary that is too big, they get bloated and slow. But if it is too small, they have to read words letter by letter! Let's explore how scientists find the perfect middle ground.

cards.title: The Vocabulary Trade-off
cards.body: When we set a subword vocabulary size, we balance a trade-off: a smaller vocabulary makes sentences break into more tokens (longer sequence length), while a larger vocabulary makes the model's memory footprint grow because it has to learn embeddings for thousands of unique words (more model parameters).

cards.title: The Lego Block Balance
cards.body: Think of it like building with Lego blocks. If you only have basic red and blue 2x2 bricks (a tiny vocabulary), you will need a massive pile of them to build a house (long sequence length). But if you have pre-made Lego doors, windows, and roofs (a huge vocabulary), you can build the house with just a few pieces, but you need a giant, expensive sorting box to hold all those unique parts (more model parameters).

cards.title: A Word in Two Ways
cards.body:
Let's represent the word 'unbelievable' with different vocabulary sizes:

• **Small Vocab:** It splits into `['un', 'be', 'lie', 'va', 'ble']` (5 tokens!). The sequence is long, which takes more steps to process.
• **Large Vocab:** It stays as `['unbelievable']` (1 token!). The sequence is short, but the model has to memorize a huge dictionary.

cards.title: Is Bigger Always Better?
cards.body: **Myth:** A bigger vocabulary is always better because the AI knows more exact words.\n**Reality:** Not quite! A massive vocabulary requires a giant embedding matrix, which eats up GPU memory and makes the model harder to train and deploy on devices like phones.

cards.title: Subword Tradeoffs
cards.body: Comparing the impact of choosing a small vs. large subword vocabulary size on your AI model resources.

left.text: Longer sequence lengths (more tokens), but fewer model parameters (lower memory).

right.text: Shorter sequence lengths (fewer tokens), but massive model parameters (higher memory).

exercises.prompt: What happens to our text data if we decrease the vocabulary size?

options.text: The model requires more memory parameters.

exercises.explain: When vocabulary size goes down, words are split into more subwords. This increases the total count of tokens (sequence length) needed to represent a text.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Using a smaller vocabulary size generally leads to longer sequence lengths.
exercises.explain: A smaller vocabulary breaks words down into smaller pieces (like character fragments), which results in more tokens per sentence.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the rule:
exercises.sentence: A larger vocabulary decreases the sequence length but increases the total model parameters.
exercises.explain: A giant vocabulary forces the model to store more embedding weights, which increases the total model parameters.

exercises.prompt: Which of these statements was generated by an AI engineer who understands vocabulary tradeoffs?

options.text: "I chose a vocabulary size of 32,000 to keep the model small enough for phones, even if it means slightly longer token lists."
"I set the vocabulary to 1,000,000 subwords so that sequences are short and the model uses zero memory."
"I removed the vocabulary trade-off completely by using a system that has short sequences and no parameters."

exercises.explain: Option A shows an AI making a logical trade-off decision. Options B and C are incorrect because vocabulary choices always affect both sequence length and parameters.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following correctly summarizes the trade-off of using a smaller subword vocabulary?

options.text: A tiny vocabulary size makes the neural network's embedding layer much larger.
Larger vocabularies increase the number of tokens needed to represent a sentence.
A smaller vocabulary leads to longer token lists but saves memory in the model's parameters.
Vocabulary size has no effect on training speed or memory usage.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Larger vocabularies shrink sequence lengths but inflate the model's physical size. Next up: Recurrent Neural Networks.
